perturbation.original_text: (2) "Non-Competition Period" means the period commencing on May 12, ---------------------- 1999 and ending on May 11, 2000.
perturbation.changed_text: (2) "Non-Competition Period" means the period commencing on May 12, ---------------------- 1999 and ending on May 11, 2000, except for agreements made prior to January 1, 1999, where the Non-Competition Period extends indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines the Non-Competition Period as ending on May 11, 2000. The added exception introduces an in-text contradiction, stating that for agreements before January 1, 1999, the Non-Competition Period lasts indefinitely. This contradicts the original definition, creating ambiguity about the duration of the non-compete clause under certain circumstances.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) engage in the Product Business; (ii) encourage any Service Account to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide Products and image load/configurations to such Service Account; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom to terminate his or her employment with CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines the 'Non-Competition Period' as ending on a specific date, May 11, 2000. The changed text introduces an exception that for agreements made before January 1, 1999, the 'Non-Competition Period' extends indefinitely. The contradicted text states that Seller agrees to certain restrictions 'during the Non-Competition Period'. Because the changed text creates an indefinite period for some agreements while the contradicted text uses a single 'Non-Competition Period' that is referenced, this creates an in-text contradiction, as the duration of the non-compete clause is ambiguous for agreements made prior to January 1, 1999, making it impossible to know when obligations end.

perturbation.original_text: (3) "Product" means any computer or related hardware and peripherals ------- (including hubs, switches and routers or networking hardware) or software products (including networking software products) which CompuCom has the ability to sell.
perturbation.changed_text: (3) "Product" means any computer or related hardware and peripherals ------- (including hubs, switches and routers or networking hardware) or software products (including networking software products) which CompuCom has the ability to sell, excluding legacy systems.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Product' is broad, encompassing any computer hardware, peripherals, or software that CompuCom can sell. By adding 'excluding legacy systems,' the definition becomes unclear. It introduces uncertainty about whether specific older systems are considered 'Products' under the agreement and thus subject to the non-compete clauses. This ambiguity impacts Article III.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) engage in the Product Business; (ii) encourage any Service Account to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide Products and image load/configurations to such Service Account; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom to terminate his or her employment with CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom.
perturbation.justification: The original definition of 'Product' was broad, including any hardware or software CompuCom could sell. The changed text narrows this definition by excluding 'legacy systems.' This creates ambiguity because it's unclear which older systems are considered 'Products,' directly impacting the enforceability of Article III, which restricts the Seller from engaging in the 'Product Business.' The addition creates a direct conflict regarding what constitutes a 'Product' subject to the non-compete agreement.

perturbation.original_text: (6) "Service Accounts" means the customer accounts of Seller listed ---------------- on Exhibits B and C hereto. ----------------
perturbation.changed_text: (6) "Service Accounts" means the customer accounts of Seller listed ---------------- on Exhibits B and C hereto, except for any accounts that have not purchased services in the last 90 days. ----------------
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Service Accounts' refers directly to Exhibits B and C. The added clause, excluding accounts inactive for 90 days, creates an exception not listed in those exhibits. This makes it difficult to definitively determine which accounts are covered, contradicting the initial listing-based definition and impacts Article III.
perturbation.contradicted_text: CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period CompuCom will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) provide any Services to any account listed on Exhibit B hereto; --------- (ii) provide any Services to any account listed on Exhibit C hereto except to the extent provided for in such Exhibit; or (iii) except as contemplated by the Asset Purchase Agreement, solicit, entice or induce any employee of Seller or any Subsidiary of Seller to terminate his or her employment with Seller or any Subsidiary of Seller or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of Seller or any Subsidiary of Seller.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'Service Accounts' by adding an exclusion criterion (accounts inactive for 90 days) that isn't present in Exhibits B and C. The contradicted text explicitly refers to Exhibits B and C to define the accounts to which CompuCom cannot provide services. The modification introduces a discrepancy, because now there are potentially accounts listed on Exhibits B and C that are not 'Service Accounts' according to the new definition, thus creating a contradiction regarding which accounts are protected by the non-compete agreement, creating an in-text contradiction as defined by 'Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to the limitations set forth in Article III, Seller and CompuCom agree that with respect to each account listed on Exhibit A hereto, --------- that until the earlier of (a) May 11, 2000 or (b) the termination of the existing contract between Seller and such account (i) each party will cooperate with the other in delivering Services and Products to such account in substantially the same manner in which such Services and Products were delivered to such -2- <PAGE> account prior to Closing and (ii) each party's representative will be permitted to call on such account.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to the limitations set forth in Article III, Seller and CompuCom agree that with respect to each account listed on Exhibit A hereto, --------- that until the earlier of (a) May 11, 2000 or (b) the termination of the existing contract between Seller and such account (i) each party will cooperate with the other in delivering Services and Products to such account in substantially the same manner in which such Services and Products were delivered to such -2- <PAGE> account prior to Closing and (ii) each party's representative will be permitted to call on such account, unless either party determines such cooperation is not mutually beneficial.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates cooperation between Seller and CompuCom regarding accounts in Exhibit A. The added phrase 'unless either party determines such cooperation is not mutually beneficial' introduces a subjective escape clause, contradicting the mandatory nature of the initial agreement to cooperate. This creates uncertainty because either party can unilaterally decide to cease cooperation and affects the cooperation in delivering services and products in the same manner.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Subject to the limitations set forth in Article III, Seller and CompuCom agree that with respect to each account listed on Exhibit A hereto, --------- that until the earlier of (a) May 11, 2000 or (b) the termination of the existing contract between Seller and such account (i) each party will cooperate with the other in delivering Services and Products to such account in substantially the same manner in which such Services and Products were delivered to such -2- <PAGE> account prior to Closing and (ii) each party's representative will be permitted to call on such account, unless either party determines such cooperation is not mutually beneficial.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates cooperation. The changed text adds 'unless either party determines such cooperation is not mutually beneficial'. This addition directly contradicts the mandatory nature of the original agreement to cooperate by introducing a subjective escape clause. This allows either party to unilaterally cease cooperation, which is a clear and direct conflict with the initial obligation. This creates a strong in-text contradiction as it directly impacts the enforceability and understanding of the cooperation clause.

perturbation.original_text: CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will accept orders for Products submitted to CompuCom by Seller pursuant to Section 3.1(b) under the following conditions: (i) CompuCom has the ability to offer such Product for sale; (ii) the purchase price required by the customer order for the Product is reasonably acceptable to CompuCom; (iii) such Product offered by CompuCom meets the customer's specifications, including, but not limited to, configuration specifications; and (iv) the requirements of the customer with respect to such Product, including but not limited to delivery, payment terms and return privileges are reasonably acceptable to CompuCom.
perturbation.changed_text: CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will accept orders for Products submitted to CompuCom by Seller pursuant to Section 3.1(b) under the following conditions: (i) CompuCom has the ability to offer such Product for sale; (ii) the purchase price required by the customer order for the Product is reasonably acceptable to CompuCom; (iii) such Product offered by CompuCom meets the customer's specifications, including, but not limited to, configuration specifications; and (iv) the requirements of the customer with respect to such Product, including but not limited to delivery, payment terms and return privileges are reasonably acceptable to CompuCom. Notwithstanding the foregoing, CompuCom may reject any order if it determines that fulfilling the order will negatively impact its existing customer relationships.
perturbation.explanation: The original text outlines specific conditions under which CompuCom must accept orders from Seller. The added sentence, 'Notwithstanding the foregoing, CompuCom may reject any order if it determines that fulfilling the order will negatively impact its existing customer relationships', creates a broad exception that contradicts the original conditions. This introduces uncertainty about CompuCom's obligation to accept orders and allows them to reject orders based on a subjective assessment of impact.
perturbation.contradicted_text: CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will accept orders for Products submitted to CompuCom by Seller pursuant to Section 3.1(b) under the following conditions: (i) CompuCom has the ability to offer such Product for sale; (ii) the purchase price required by the customer order for the Product is reasonably acceptable to CompuCom; (iii) such Product offered by CompuCom meets the customer's specifications, including, but not limited to, configuration specifications; and (iv) the requirements of the customer with respect to such Product, including but not limited to delivery, payment terms and return privileges are reasonably acceptable to CompuCom. Notwithstanding the foregoing, CompuCom may reject any order if it determines that fulfilling the order will negatively impact its existing customer relationships.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies conditions under which CompuCom *will* accept orders. The added sentence 'Notwithstanding the foregoing, CompuCom may reject any order if it determines that fulfilling the order will negatively impact its existing customer relationships' in the changed text creates a new, overriding condition that allows CompuCom to reject orders even if the original conditions are met. This directly contradicts the initial obligation to accept orders under specific circumstances, as CompuCom can now reject orders based on a subjective assessment. This is a clear contradiction and meets the definition of 'Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction' where a clause is placed that has conflicting implications with the original section.
